The high-level architecture connects the Spryker DCE customer portal to downstream enterprise systems through Azure-native iPaaS services, avoiding custom middleware. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0002][chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0003][chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0004]

Components are organised into logical zones: Spryker customer portal on the left, Azure integration services in the centre, and downstream enterprise systems on the right. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0012][chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0013][chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0010]

Key Azure platform services are:
1. Azure APIM as the single Spryker-facing API gateway for authentication, throttling, routing, schema validation, monitoring, and developer portal. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0012]
2. Logic Apps and Azure Functions for orchestration, transformation, connector-based workflows, custom HTTP integrations, and durable orchestrations. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0012]
3. Service Bus for asynchronous queues, topics, retries, dead-lettering, long-running transactions, and decoupling. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0012]
4. Event Grid for event distribution from D365 and other systems to Spryker webhooks. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0012]
5. Data Factory, Application Insights, and Azure AD B2C for batch sync, observability, and customer identity. [chunk-kb-sample-document-with-image-1_doc-dce-integration-platform-implementation-plan_chunk-0012]

retrieved_chunks.chunk_text:
4. High-level architecture

The diagram below presents the high-level architecture. Components are organised into four logical zones: the Spryker customer portal (left), the Azure integration services that constitute the platform (centre), and the downstream enterprise systems organised by category (right).

Figure 1: High-level integration architecture

1. Executive summary

This document specifies the implementation plan for the integration layer connecting the Spryker-based DCE customer portal to nine enterprise systems across two Azure tenants. The architecture is built on Azure native iPaaS services — API Management (APIM) as the gateway, Logic Apps and Functions for orchestration, Service Bus for asynchronous messaging, Event Grid for event-driven push, Data Factory for batch synchronisation, Application Insights for observability, and Azure AD B2C for customer identity.

The approach intentionally avoids building a custom middleware application. Instead, it leverages Microsoft-maintained PaaS services, which already provide the platform capabilities (pluggable connectors, multi-protocol support, identity federation, orchestration, monitoring) that a custom middleware would need to reproduce. This aligns to Ricoh's existing Azure enterprise standard and Microsoft's reference integration architecture, eliminates vendor platform lock-in, and provides a sustainable foundation for future integrations beyond the customer portal scope.

The plan is structured around a three-phase rollout: a Foundation phase (2–4 weeks) to establish landing-zone and governance; a Pilot phase (3 months, aligned to the Validation SOW) delivering the first end-to-end D365 F&O integration; and Expansion phases (6–12 months) onboarding the remaining systems following the same patterns. The estimated three-year total cost of ownership is materially lower than the custom middleware alternative, primarily because the integration platform itself does not require building or maintaining.

Critical success factors include: (a) securing Azure iPaaS expertise via a Microsoft Gold Partner or internal Azure capability, (b) investing in upfront architecture and governance design before building integrations, and (c) per-system fit validation of pre-built connectors against specific use cases. Risks are surfaced and mitigation strategies are proposed.

4.1 Component overview

**Component** | **Role** | **Service tier (initial)**
----
Azure APIM | Single API gateway facing Spryker. Handles authentication, throttling, policy-based routing (including tenant routing), schema validation, monitoring, and developer portal. | Developer or Standard (Premium if VNet integration is required)
Logic Apps + Functions | Orchestration and transformation. Logic Apps for connector-based workflows (400+ pre-built connectors); Functions for custom HTTP integrations and durable orchestrations. | Logic Apps Standard plan; Functions Consumption plan
Azure Service Bus | Asynchronous message broker with queues, topics, retry policies, and dead-letter queues. Used for long-running transactions and decoupling. | Standard tier
Azure Event Grid | Event distribution. Receives business events from D365 and other systems and pushes them to Spryker webhook receivers, eliminating the need for polling. | Basic
Azure Data Factory | Batch and scheduled data movement, primarily for Snowflake data warehouse synchronisation. | Standard
Application Insights | End-to-end observability: distributed tracing, performance metrics, dependency mapping, alerting. | Per-GB ingestion
Azure AD B2C | Customer identity provider. Handles authentication, registration, and federation. Cross-tenant identity service. | MAU-based pricing

Contents

1.  Executive summary

2.  Background and objectives

3.  Architectural principles

4.  High-level architecture

5.  Component detailed design

6.  Multi-tenant strategy

7.  Integration patterns by system

8.  Integration scenarios (end-to-end walkthroughs)

9.  Cross-cutting concerns

10. Resource and skills requirements

11. Execution plan and phases

12. Development workflow

13. Risk and mitigation

14. Success metrics

15. Appendix

Pattern characteristics

•

Pattern:

Inbound event with fan-out via Event Grid pub/sub

•

Inbound surface:

MPS Platform posts to a dedicated APIM webhook endpoint; APIM validates signature and authenticates the source

•

Fan-out:

Event Grid delivers the event to multiple Logic App subscribers in parallel, each handling its responsibility independently

•

Decoupled processing:

failure of one subscriber (e.g., ServiceNow unavailable) does not affect others; failed deliveries retried per-subscriber

•

Custom HTTP integration:

MPS is a Ricoh-internal proprietary system without a Logic Apps connector; custom HTTP endpoints in the Logic App handle MPS-specific request signing

Volume considerations

If the device fleet grows to thousands of devices reporting continuously, the inbound surface may need to migrate to Azure IoT Hub for higher ingestion throughput. Event Grid + Logic Apps subscribers downstream remain unchanged — only the ingestion endpoint shifts. This is a scaling consideration to address in Phase 3.
